title: The Difference Between Integration and Spiritual Materialism
body:
The Difference Between Integration and Spiritual Materialism

Let’s get straight to the point: if you’re on a journey involving psychedelics, meditation, or any form of deep inner work, you need to understand the difference between integration and spiritual materialism. Because if you don’t, you might think you’re growing when, in fact, you’re stuck in a loop that keeps you small.

Here’s the thing, spiritual materialism sounds fancy and feels good. It’s that temptation to treat spiritual growth like a shopping list or a trophy case. You collect experiences, badges, or identities, but underneath, the real work isn’t happening. Integration, by contrast, is messy, quiet, and often invisible. It’s the slow folding of new insights into your daily life, your relationships, your struggles.

What Is Integration, Really?

When you talk about integration, I want you to think about that moment after a psychedelic trip when the lights come back on. You’re not just replaying the visions or insights like a highlight reel. Instead, you’re asking questions like: How do I live differently now? What patterns did I see in myself that I want to change? How do I hold this new awareness without turning it into a show?

Integration is about living your insights. It means you don’t just say “I saw God” or “I’m enlightened now.” You show up differently. You deal with your anger without blaming others. You have hard conversations. You breathe through discomfort instead of escaping it. Integration requires humility and patience.

For example, take someone like Maya, a woman I met during a retreat in Oregon. After her first experience with psilocybin, she was convinced she’d “figured it all out.” She started telling everyone she was “woke” and stopped going to therapy because she thought she didn’t need it anymore. Six months later, Maya was back in the same loops of anxiety and self-doubt. What happened? She skipped the integration part.

True integration is often a slow burn. It’s about practice over flash. It’s waking up and choosing kindness when you’d rather lash out. It’s crying in front of a friend without dressing it up in spiritual jargon. It’s messy, imperfect, and utterly real.

What Is Spiritual Materialism?

Spiritual materialism was a term coined by Chögyam Trungpa Rinpoche, a Tibetan Buddhist teacher. He warned about the temptation to turn spirituality into another form of ego inflation. Instead of breaking down your walls, you build new ones - made of spiritual experiences, exotic practices, or “higher” knowledge.

Look, spiritual materialism is everywhere. It’s the person who posts their latest meditation retreat with a filter and a quote about “transcendence” but can’t sit with their own boredom for five minutes. It’s the person who brags about microdosing but never actually does the inner work needed to change their patterns. It’s the “spiritual influencer” who sells you a lifestyle rather than tools for healing.

Does that land? Here’s a practical example: I once worked with a client named Jason. After reading The Psychedelic Explorer's Guide (paid link), he was eager to dive into microdosing and psychedelics. But instead of sitting with the discomfort his experiences brought up, he started collecting every new technique, every new supplement, every new workshop. He was building a spiritual toolbox but never opening it. His ego loved the idea of being “advanced,” but his emotional wounds stayed untouched.

Why Does This Matter to You?

If you’re serious about your journey, this distinction is everything. You want to avoid spiritual materialism because it traps you in a loop of self-deception. You want to embrace integration because that’s where real change happens. Not just for a moment, but for your whole life.

Integration asks you to slow down and face your humanity. It asks you to wear your growth as an act of service, not a badge of honor. It means moving beyond the “trip” or the “experience” and living the lessons every day.

Here’s a question for you: When was the last time you sat with your pain without trying to fix it with another technique or quote? When was the last time you admitted, “I don’t know how to be better right now,” and just stayed with that?

Concrete Steps to Choose Integration Over Spiritual Materialism

Honestly, integration isn’t some mystical force you wait for. It’s a set of actions and attitudes you choose. Here are some practical ways to start:

  • Journal with honesty. Instead of writing what you think you “should” feel or what sounds enlightened, write your raw thoughts and emotions. No judgment, no editing.
  • Build a daily routine that supports embodiment. This could be as simple as 5 minutes of breathwork, 10 minutes of mindful movement, or using an acupressure mat and pillow set (paid link) to help your body release tension and stay grounded.
  • Seek out real community. Find people who hold space for your unpolished self, not just your spiritual highlights. This might be a support group, a therapist, or a trusted friend.
  • Practice patience. Growth isn’t linear. You’ll take two steps forward and one back. The key is to keep coming back to the work without shame.
  • Read with intention. Books like Stealing Fire (paid link) can inspire you to understand altered states, but remember to bring those lessons into your body and relationships, not just your head.

Spotting the Red Flags of Spiritual Materialism

How do you know if you’re slipping into spiritual materialism? Watch for these signs:

  • You’re collecting experiences but not changing behavior. Going to many retreats or reading many books but still reacting the same way in stressful situations.
  • You use spiritual language to avoid real feelings. Saying “I’m just here to love” when you’re actually avoiding conflict or pain.
  • You compare your progress to others. “I’m more awakened than them” or “I’m less enlightened.” This shows the ego is still in charge.
  • You resist discomfort. Growth almost always involves discomfort. If you’re only chasing good vibes, you’re missing the point.

One way to catch yourself is to ask: Am I trying to show off my spirituality or live it quietly? That’s a good checkpoint.

Why Integration Is a Lifelong Practice

Look, integration doesn’t end after your first - or fiftieth - psychedelic trip. It’s a lifelong practice. You’ll hit plateaus and breakthroughs, moments of clarity and times when you feel lost. That’s normal. It’s human.

Truth is, integration asks you to bring your whole self to the process: your fears, your hopes, your anger, your joy. It’s not about escaping who you are or trying to be someone “higher.” It’s about showing up for yourself with honesty, day after day.

If you want a deep dive into how trauma and the body affect your journey, I highly recommend reading The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). It’s a crucial read that will help you understand why integration can feel so hard - and why it’s worth it.

The Science and Stories Behind It

There’s growing research showing that psychedelic experiences alone don’t guarantee healing. According to studies published on Nature.com, the integration process - how you process and apply the experience afterward - is what determines long-term benefits.

That means no magic trip will fix your life if you don’t do the work afterward. It’s like planting a seed but never watering it.

A Final Thought

If you’re feeling overwhelmed by all the spiritual advice, retreats, books, and “hacks,” take a breath. Be kind to yourself. Integration isn’t another thing to add to your to-do list. It’s about how you show up to your life right now, flaws and all.

If you want to learn more about how to ground your psychedelic experiences into everyday healing, you can always check out my other article Integration Practices That Actually Work. And for ongoing support, you can visit my site at kalesh.love.

Keep it real, keep it honest. You’ve got this.

Disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. Always consult a qualified healthcare provider before making changes to your health regimen.
